You engage the drive. Stars stretch to lines. The Sable Moth leaps into the void, and the compass pulses warm and certain in your hand, pointing the way. You found the artefact. You made an ally. You followed a ghost of a map to the edge of known space and found something that changes what the map means.

The Empire will come eventually. Malachar or someone like him — the Bureau does not forget, and it does not forgive. But not today.

Today, you are ahead of them. And the compass knows the way.

end_resistance.text:
Voss finds you in the cockpit. He makes a call on his encrypted comm — three digits, a frequency Zara gave you in a tea stall on a dying station. You did not think anyone was listening on the other end. Someone was.

Three hours out from New Babylon, a ship drops out of FTL alongside you. Old hull, no markings, running dark. It matches velocity and holds position off your port side like it has done this before.

They want the compass. Not to sell it. To use it — to put Precursor navigation technology into hands that will not burn worlds with it. Voss looks at you across the cockpit.

'Your call,' he says.

The compass pulses in your hand — warm, patient, ancient. Zara said it should end up somewhere that uses it.

choices.text: Hand it over — let the cause have it
Keep it — but stay in contact

end_resistance_give.text:
The airlock cycles. A woman in an unmarked flight suit takes the compass from your hands with a nod — no speeches, no ceremony. She holds it the way a navigator holds a chart: with professional care and no sentiment.

You watch the compass go without regret. The silver veins pulse once against the woman's gloved palm, then settle. It knows it is going somewhere useful.

The unmarked ship breaks away and jumps. You and Voss sit in the cockpit in the silence that follows. The nav console is empty. Your hands are light.

'Feel strange?' Voss asks.

'No,' you say. And mean it.

Some things are worth more than what you can sell them for. You and Voss fly on, lighter and better, into a galaxy that just got a little more complicated for the Empire.

You survived. You found the artefact. And you gave it away.

That might be the best thing you have ever done.

end_resistance_keep.text:
You keep the compass — but you share the coordinates. Everything the compass showed you, every frequency, every jump point to the dead star and the signal beyond it. The unmarked ship records it all.

A compromise. The cause gets what it needs. You keep what you found.

Voss watches the data transfer complete and smiles — tight, tired, real. 'Good enough,' he says.

The unmarked ship breaks away and jumps. You sit in the cockpit with the compass warm in your hand, its silver veins pulsing with that ancient, patient light. The coordinates are shared now. The Empire's monopoly on Precursor knowledge cracked open by a scavenger, a deserter, and a woman who ran a tea stall.

You engage the drive. The frontier awaits. And for the first time in a long time, the word 'frontier' feels like it means something.

You survived. You kept the artefact. And you did not keep it to yourself.

That will have to be enough. It is.

end_survivor.text:
Voss finds you in the cockpit as the FTL drive spools up. He has a fresh bandage on his arm and a look on his face that you have not seen before — something close to peace.

'Where are we going?' he asks.

'Anywhere that isn't here.'

He settles into the co-pilot's seat like someone who has needed a co-pilot's seat for a long time. His hands find the console controls with the ease of old training — Imperial Navy, you remember. Before everything went wrong.

You engage the drive. Stars stretch to lines. The Sable Moth leaps into the void.

In your hand, the Void Compass pulses — warm, alive, ancient. It contains star charts to places no living being has visited in ten thousand years. Precursor worlds. Lost civilisations. The kind of discoveries that change the course of history.

But that is a story for another day. Today, you survived. You found the artefact. You made an ally. And somewhere behind you, the Empire is learning that not everything in the galaxy can be taken by force.

You lean back in the pilot's chair and watch the stars burn. Beside you, Voss does the same.

end_lone_wolf.text:
You engage the FTL drive alone. Stars stretch to lines. The Sable Moth shudders once, then leaps into the void, leaving New Babylon Station as a dwindling point of light in the rear viewport.

In your hand, the Void Compass pulses — warm, alive, ancient. It contains star charts to places no living being has visited in ten thousand years. Precursor worlds. Lost civilisations. Treasure beyond counting.

You are alone, as always. But you are alive, and you are rich, and the Empire can choke on its own arrogance.

You set a course for the frontier. There is a fence on Tellus IV who deals in Precursor artefacts and does not ask questions. After that — well. The Compass is a map. And you have always wondered what lies beyond the edge of known space.

The stars burn cold and bright. The void awaits.

end_pyrrhic.text:
You escaped. You are alive. The station recedes in the viewport, lit up with Imperial activity — searchlights sweeping the hull, patrol craft launching from the docking ring, comm channels crackling with cordon orders. They are tearing through every deck looking for you, and they will find evidence of everything you did down there. The dead soldiers. The disrupted sensors. The trail of a scavenger who fought instead of running.

Malachar will not forget this. The Bureau does not forget. Your face will be in their files by morning, your ship's signature flagged at every port from here to the Core.

The Void Compass pulses in your hand. You have what you came for. But the person who ran the diversion that bought you your exit is not on this ship. Voss. You do not know if he made it out, or if he is in Imperial custody, or if the station took him the way it takes everything else eventually.

You set a course. The stars burn cold and indifferent. You survived. That used to be enough.

It is harder to be sure of that now.

death_extraction.text:
You reached Voss. That was the easy part. Getting out again — that was the part you did not plan for.

The cordon closed behind you. Three soldiers became six. Six became a full squad. They came from both ends of the corridor, cutting off the junction, and the mathematics of the situation became very simple.

Voss fought beside you. He was good — better than you expected, clean shots, disciplined movement. But there were too many of them, and the corridor was too narrow, and eventually a stun round found him and then one found you.

The Void Compass rolls from your hand as you fall. Its silver veins pulse once, twice, then go dark.

Malachar arrives three minutes later. He picks up the compass without looking at either of you. An aide takes notes.

Your adventure ends here.

e_pre_1.text:
You follow the waypoint left, but after thirty metres the corridor changes.

The frost stops. Not gradually — it ends in a clean line across the floor, as if the cold knows better than to go further. Beyond that line the walls are smooth dark metal, seamless, without bolt or weld. The ceiling is higher than it should be. The proportions are wrong in a way you can't immediately name.

Your boots echo differently here. The sound doesn't bounce — it drops, swallowed by surfaces that weren't designed for human acoustics.

There is a side passage to your left. It has no door. The opening is shaped like a tall, narrow oval, its edges bevelled inward. Through it, faintly, you can see light. Not station light. Something cooler, steadier, with no visible source.

choices.text: Step through the oval passage toward the light
Stay on course — follow the waypoint to Research Lab 7

e_pre_2.text:
The passage is short — four steps — but the proportions make it feel longer. The oval opening is wide enough for you to walk through without turning sideways, but only just. It was sized for something narrower at the shoulder and taller at the crown.

You emerge into a room.

It is not large. Roughly octagonal, perhaps ten metres across, though the angles are not quite regular — some walls are slightly longer than others, giving the space an asymmetry that feels deliberate. The floor is the same dark metal, but here it slopes. Not toward a wall. Toward the centre, where a shallow depression holds a drain or channel of some kind. The channel leads down at an angle that does not correspond to the station's gravity.

The light comes from the ceiling. No fixture, no panel. The metal itself holds a faint luminescence, blue-white, constant. It has been doing this for a very long time. You are certain of that without knowing why.

Against the far wall, there are recesses. Shallow niches set into the metal at varying heights. Some are at chest level. Others are near the floor. Their shapes suggest they once held objects, but the objects are gone. The niches have too many curves — sized for things with geometries your hands wouldn't know how to grip.

choices.text: Examine the recesses more closely
Follow the channel in the floor — see where it leads

e_pre_3.text:
You crouch beside one of the lower niches and hold your hand inside it. The surface is warm. Not from any mechanism you can detect — no vibration, no hum. The warmth is in the metal itself, as though it remembers being held.

The niche's shape is complex. Five lobes, but they branch at the tips, and the branches fork again. Whatever rested here had a structure more like coral than bone. You try to imagine the hand — the limb — the body that would reach for this shelf at this height, at this angle, and your mind slides off the picture.

In the niche beside it, higher up, you find something. A thin disc of the same dark metal, no wider than your palm. It is lodged in the back of the recess as though it slipped behind whatever was meant to sit there. You pry it loose.

It weighs almost nothing. One face is smooth. The other has a pattern of raised lines — not writing, not circuitry. Something between the two. The lines converge toward one edge of the disc, all of them, like rivers flowing toward a coast you can't see.

choices.text: Follow the channel in the floor before you leave
Return to the main corridor and head for Research Lab 7

e_pre_4.text:
The channel in the floor is shallow — barely deep enough to hold water, if water is what it was meant to hold. It runs from the central depression to the far wall, where it passes through a slit in the metal and continues beyond.

You follow it to the wall and kneel. Through the slit, you can see the channel continuing — sloping down, curving left, descending into the station's deeper structure. Or into whatever the station was built on top of.

The light is stronger here. You press your face close to the slit and look.

Below — far below, further than the station's hull should allow — there is a space. You can't judge the size. The light from the channel illuminates only the nearest surfaces: more dark metal, more curves, more geometry that doesn't repeat. But the sense of volume is enormous. The air that comes through the slit is dry and cold and carries no smell at all.

You are looking at the top of something. Whatever this room is, it is the uppermost point of a structure that extends downward into a space the station maps don't show.

The light below is not diffuse. It comes from a direction. You turn your head and the angle resolves: it comes from galactic north-east. From a point in the sky that, at this station's position, holds nothing charted.

choices.text: Examine the wall recesses before you leave
You've seen enough — return to the corridor and find Research Lab 7

e_pre_5.text:
You step back through the oval passage into the main corridor. The frost is waiting on the other side of that clean line. Station light. Station sounds. The ticking of thermal expansion in the hull plates.

You stand still for a moment. The waypoint arrow on your data chip is patient, pointing ahead toward Research Lab 7. The Void Compass is close.

But you are thinking about the light. The direction it came from. The way every line in that room pointed the same way, like a compass needle that was never meant to spin.

choices.text: Continue to Research Lab 7

b_wit_1.text:
You don't take the direct route. Instead you move along the container stacks, keeping low, working your way through the maze of freight modules toward the far side of the bay. Longer path. Better cover.

You hear them before you see them. Boots on deck plating — not running, not hurried. A measured pace. The sound of people who have a schedule.

You press yourself into the gap between two shipping containers and look through the slit where their edges don't quite meet.

Three soldiers in Bureau grey. Standard sidearms, holstered. They're not expecting a fight. Between them, a small group — a woman in maintenance coveralls, an older man with a station tech badge, a kid who can't be more than sixteen. Residents. The kind of people who live in cargo bays because they can't afford the upper decks.

The soldiers are walking them toward the freight elevator at the far end of the bay. Nobody is in restraints. Nobody is being dragged. They're just walking.

choices.text: Keep watching

b_wit_2.text:
The woman in coveralls stops. She turns to the nearest soldier — a young man, clean-shaven, unremarkable.

'We haven't done anything,' she says. Not shouting. Not pleading. Just stating it, the way you'd correct a clerical error. 'We live here. We work Bay 14 cargo sorting. We don't know anything about Precursor artefacts.'

The soldier looks at her. He nods, once, the way you'd acknowledge someone telling you the time. Then he puts his hand on her shoulder — not roughly — and guides her forward. She goes. There is nothing else to do.

The older man says something you can't hear. The soldier behind him answers. You catch the tail end of it: '...processing area. Shouldn't take long.'

Processing. That's the word. Not detention, not arrest. Processing. Like cargo. Like inventory being moved to a different bay before audit.

The kid is the last one through the elevator doors. He looks back over his shoulder, scanning the cargo bay — not for rescue, just looking at the place where he lives, as if he is trying to remember it. The doors close.

The Void Compass sits in your pack. You are aware of its weight in a way you weren't before.

choices.text: There's nothing you can do here. Move on.
Follow the soldiers toward the freight elevator

b_wit_3.text:
You move to the freight elevator. The indicator light shows it descending — sub-level 2, sub-level 3. It stops at sub-level 4. A storage tier. Nothing down there but empty containers and station infrastructure.

Nothing down there that anyone would need to see.

You stand at the elevator doors for a moment longer than you should. You could call it back. Ride it down. Find them. And then what. Fight three armed soldiers. Rescue three people you don't know to a station that is being locked down by an Imperial Inquisitor with a warship at the dock.

You are a scavenger. You survive by knowing what you can carry and what you have to leave behind.

You turn away from the elevator and head for the exit.

choices.text: Continue

e_pre_clean.text:
In the antechamber, you saw the same channels cut into the same stone — the same light-carrying geometry. You understand, without being able to explain how, exactly where the pressure plate’s trigger point is. Not learned. Recognised.

Your hand goes to the right spot without hesitation. You press, and the plate clicks inert.

You lift the Void Compass from the pedestal. It is warm before you touch it. The silver veins pulse brighter, and for a moment you feel the same pull you felt in the antechamber — that sense of direction, pointing somewhere specific. It is stronger now. Much stronger.

choices.text: Let the compass show you what it wants to show you

h_shaft_3c.text:
You kneel beside the dead scavenger. She has been here a long time — months, maybe longer. The dry station air has preserved her face in a hollow grimace, lips pulled back from teeth.

Her gear harness is stripped, as you noted, but whoever cleaned her out missed a few things. A folded sheet of station blueprint, tucked inside her boot. You pull it free and smooth it out. Hand-annotated in a cramped, precise hand. She mapped her route through the shafts — the coolant breach, the crawler nests, a flooded section she marked AVOID. Her final annotation is a circled section of Sublevel Nine with three words: COMPASS IS REAL.

She believed. She came this far alone, and she believed.

Something else. Written on the back of the blueprint in different handwriting — blocky, masculine: LOOKING FOR THE SAME THING. DON'T TRUST THE BUREAU. The ink is fresher than her annotations. Someone else found her body, read her map, and left a message for whoever came next.

You fold the blueprint and pocket it. The dead scavenger stares at the ceiling with empty sockets. She got close. Closer than most.

choices.text: Search the equipment bay more thoroughly
Take the passage back and continue the descent

h_shaft_7b.text:
Before committing to the collapsed section, you notice a side hatch in the corridor wall — a maintenance access point, its signage still legible: ENG. RECORDS — SUBLEVEL EIGHT. The hatch is ajar.

Beyond it, a narrow room lined with filing drawers and a wall-mounted terminal. The terminal is dead, but the drawers are intact. You pull one open. Paper records — actual paper, yellowed and brittle. Engineering reports from the station's original construction crew, decades before the Imperial garrison arrived.

Most of it is technical. Weld specifications. Load-bearing calculations. But one folder is different. It is labelled ANOMALOUS MATERIALS — SECTOR 7 LOWER and stamped with a classification mark you do not recognise. Inside, a single page: a memo requesting that Research Lab 7 be sealed with reinforced bulkheads and placed under separate environmental controls. The reason given is one word: CONTAINMENT.

Whatever the Void Compass is, the people who built this station knew it was here. They built walls around it.

choices.text: Take the collapsed left branch — tight but direct
Take the clear right branch — longer but safer

h_shaft_rest.text:
The rest area is small — four bunks bolted to the wall, a fold-down table, a chemical stove that still has a canister of fuel. A layer of dust covers everything. No one has been here in years.

The bunks are stripped. Personal effects cleared out in the evacuation. But someone left a logbook on the table — a hardbound Station Authority issue, its cover embossed with a serial number. The last pages are filled with tight, careful handwriting.

There is also a sealed ration pack wedged behind one of the bunks. Still vacuum-sealed. Still good, if the preservatives have held.

choices.text: Read the logbook
Leave the rest area and continue the descent

h_shaft_rest_log.text:
The logbook belongs to Maintenance Chief Rhul Okafor. Most entries are mundane — shift rosters, repair schedules, requisition forms. But the final week of entries is different.

'Station-wide memo: all non-essential personnel to evacuate to upper decks. Imperial garrison withdrawing. No explanation given. Research staff in Lab 7 left two days ago without filing departure logs.'

'We sealed the lower decks as ordered. Full atmospheric shutdown below Sublevel Ten. I asked Commander Yeoh why. She said it was classified. She looked scared. I have never seen Yeoh scared of anything.'

The last entry: 'Something is wrong with the air recyclers on Nine. They are pulling more power than they should — drawing from the emergency grid. The sealed sections below should be vacuum by now, but the atmospheric readings show breathable air in Lab 7. That is not possible unless something down there is generating its own atmosphere.'

Below that, in a different pen: 'We are leaving. All of us. If anyone reads this — do not go below Sublevel Ten.'

You close the logbook and set it back on the table.

choices.text: Leave the rest area and continue the descent

h_shaft_5b.text:
The transition between levels is marked by a change in the air. It grows colder with each step, and the mould on the walls thickens — dark patches of it climbing the conduit runs like something reaching upward. Your breath fogs.

The shaft narrows. The ceiling lowers. You are moving through the spaces between the station's bones now, where the builders never intended anyone to walk. Cable runs press against your shoulders. The floor is uneven — grating in some places, bare hull plate in others, with gaps that show the dark of the level below.

Your headlamp catches something scratched into the wall at eye level. Three vertical lines, crossed by a diagonal. A scavenger's mark — it means PASSAGE CLEAR, ROUTE TESTED. Someone came this way before you and survived it. Small comfort, but comfort nonetheless.

choices.text: Continue the descent

h_shaft_voss_warn.text:
Voss grabs your arm. His grip is iron.

'Stop,' he breathes. Not a suggestion. An order.

You stop. He points — not ahead, but up. Your headlamp follows his finger to the ceiling, where a pale shape clings motionless to a pipe junction. A duct crawler, large, its body pressed flat against the metal in ambush posture. You would have walked directly under it.

'Saw the leg move,' Voss whispers. 'They drop on you from above. Go for the neck.' He draws his pistol and sights along the barrel. 'On three. One, two—'

He fires. The shot is deafening in the enclosed shaft. The crawler drops, twitching, and Voss stamps on it twice with his boot heel. Efficient. Brutal.

'You're welcome,' he says, holstering the pistol. The other crawlers — you can hear them deeper in the shaft — scatter at the sound. For now.

The way forward is clear.

choices.text: Continue through the cleared shaft

h_shaft_4c.text:
You pause at the edge of the flooded section. The body your foot caught on is just visible beneath the surface — a dark shape in the luminescent coolant, face down, arms spread. A scavenger's headlamp, still faintly glowing, rests on the bottom beside the body like a fallen star.

The coolant has preserved the body better than the dry air preserved the woman in the equipment bay. This one is more recent. Weeks, not months. Male. Heavy build. His gear harness is intact — no one stripped him. He came this far and simply stopped.

You can see the cause. His right leg, below the knee, is swollen to twice its normal size — dark, mottled, the skin split. A crawler bite, badly infected. He made it this far on adrenaline and willpower and then the leg gave out. He fell face-first into the coolant and did not get up.

His pack is submerged but within reach. You could fish it out, but it would mean kneeling in the coolant — more exposure to the cold chemicals than you have already endured.

test_luck.lucky_text: You plunge your arm into the coolant and hook the pack's strap. The cold bites deep but you drag it free. Inside: waterlogged ration bars, a corroded multi-tool, and a hand-drawn map of the lower shafts. The map is ruined, but from what you can read, it confirms your route. You wring the worst of the coolant from your sleeve and press on, grateful for the confirmation.
test_luck.unlucky_text: You reach for the pack and lose your balance. One knee plunges into the coolant. The cold is savage — it locks your muscles and you gasp, inhaling acrid fumes. You drag yourself back, empty-handed. The pack is wedged under the body and won't come free. You sit on the grating, shaking, and wait for the feeling to return to your leg. When it does, it comes as pain.

h_shaft_9b.text:
Before descending further, you notice a side passage branching from the main shaft — a narrow crawlway marked with yellow hazard stripes. It ends after ten metres at a reinforced viewport set into the floor. A window looking down.

You kneel and wipe the grime from the glass. Below, lit by the blue emergency lighting of the lower decks, you can see a corridor stretching into the distance. The walls are different down there — darker metal, smoother, without the crude welds and exposed conduit of the upper station. Purpose-built. The architecture of people who expected what they built to last centuries.

Something moves in the corridor below. A figure — humanoid, armoured. It passes beneath the viewport and continues out of sight. Grey combat armour. Imperial issue.

Malachar's advance team. They are already in the lower decks.

You pull back from the viewport. The glass is cold against your palms. Somewhere below you, armed professionals are searching for the same thing you are. And they have a head start.

choices.text: Descend into the condemned sections

h_9e.text:
You work your way along a row of intact containers on the bay's north wall. These are smaller units — personnel storage, personal effects. The kind of containers people fill when they are leaving a place and do not expect to come back.

One is open. Inside, the remnants of someone's life: folded clothes, a toolkit, a framed photograph of two children standing in front of what looks like a docking bay window with stars behind them. The photo is faded but the children are smiling. They do not know that one day this station will be sealed and abandoned.

Another container holds engineering manuals and a set of calibration instruments, all carefully packed. A third is empty except for a single item — a Station Authority winter coat, heavy-duty, rated for the cold sections of the lower decks. You pull it on over your gear harness. It fits poorly but the warmth is immediate.

The bay stretches on. The loading tunnel waits at the far end.

choices.text: Return to the centre of the bay

h_shaft_1b.text:
Voss holds up a fist — the military signal to halt. You stop. He tilts his head, listening, then moves to the wall and runs his fingers along a section of conduit housing.

'Here,' he says quietly. He pulls a loose panel free, revealing a recessed terminal behind the wall cladding. The screen is cracked but still drawing power — a maintenance monitoring station, hidden from casual view. It shows atmospheric readings for the lower levels.

'Look at this.' He taps the screen. 'Sublevel Ten — atmosphere should be zero. Station Authority sealed it and pumped the air. But these readings show breathable atmosphere in sections of the lower deck. Someone — or something — is maintaining life support down there.'

He replaces the panel carefully. 'Malachar's scouts would have found this too. He knows what's down there is still active.' He checks his pistol. 'We should keep moving. Quietly.'

choices.text: Take the main descent route to the left
Explore the service passage to the right

h_shaft_voss_rest.text:
At the bottom of the final ladder, Voss leans against the wall and lets out a long breath. The tension in his shoulders eases for the first time since you met him. He pulls a dented canteen from his kit and drinks, then offers it to you.

'I keep thinking about the Qeth team,' he says, not looking at you. 'The archaeologists Malachar killed. There was a woman — Dr. Sev, their lead researcher. She spent three years mapping Precursor sites across the sector. She told me once that the Precursors didn't build things to be found. They built things to be ready. As if they knew someone would need them, eventually.'

He caps the canteen. 'The Void Compass isn't just valuable, is what I'm saying. If Dr. Sev was right — and she was right about everything else — it does something. Something Malachar wants very badly.'

He straightens up. 'Let's find it before he does.'

choices.text: Continue to the main junction

h_9b.text:
You skirt the east wall of the bay, keeping your headlamp low. The containers here are stacked three high, their markings still legible: PATEL TRADING CONSORTIUM — BONDED CARGO — NEW BABYLON STATION. Commercial goods, locked in place when the lower decks were sealed.

Most are intact. But one stack, near the loading tunnel, has been disturbed. The top container sits at an angle, shoved aside by something enormously strong. The middle container is torn open from the inside — the reinforced wall peeled outward like a flower blooming in steel. Claw marks score the metal in long parallel grooves.

Inside the ruined container, a nest. Shredded insulation material piled deep, stained dark with old fluids. Bones — rat-sized, human-sized, and everything between — are scattered through the bedding. The smell is thick and feral.

This is where the stalker sleeps. You are standing in its home.

choices.text: Search the nest carefully
Back away quietly

h_9c.text:
You hold your breath and pick through the stalker's nest. It is deeply unpleasant work. The bedding is warm — body heat, recently occupied. The creature is close.

Among the bones and shredded material, you find objects that do not belong. A Station Authority badge, corroded but legible: the name reads SGT. K. MWANGI. A broken comm unit, its casing cracked by teeth marks. A child's shoe, impossibly small, impossibly out of place in this dead section of the station.

The creature was human once. These are the things it kept. Some buried instinct, deeper than the mutation, made it collect fragments of the life it lost.

You put the objects back where you found them. There is nothing useful here — only grief, old and calcified. But the nest tells you something practical: the stalker returns here. If you move quickly toward the loading tunnel, you will have to pass through its hunting ground.

choices.text: Press on toward the loading tunnel
Go back to the main bay

h_10b.text:
You move toward the loading tunnel slowly, reading the bay as you go. The signs are everywhere once you know what to look for. Scratches on the floor — long gouges where something heavy dragged itself between the container rows. A dark smear on a container wall at head height, too thick to be oil. The rank biological smell grows stronger with every step.

You find a kill site. A rat — one of the station's ubiquitous vermin, grown fat in the lower decks — has been torn apart and partially consumed. The remains are fresh. Hours old at most.

The stalker is active. It is awake and it has fed recently, which means it is either sated and sluggish or energised and territorial. You have no way to know which.

Ahead, between you and the loading tunnel, the container rows open into a wide clearing. Good sightlines, but nowhere to hide. If the creature is here, this is where you will meet it.

choices.text: Cross the clearing
Circle back to the main bay

h_9d.text:
You draw the plasma pistol and check the charge. The indicator glows a steady amber — three-quarter power. Down here, in the close confines of the cargo bay, the weapon gives you an edge nothing else in your kit can match. The stalker is big, but superheated plasma does not care about size.

The pistol's barrel emits a faint warmth against your palm. You keep it drawn as you move between the containers, its weight a reassurance in the cold dark. Whatever is down here, you are not meeting it with just a knife.

The loading tunnel lies ahead. The smell is stronger now — rank and animal.

choices.text: Follow the stalker's trail toward the tunnel
Press directly to the loading tunnel

s_market_1.text:
You drift deeper into the market, past the main stalls. The crowd thins slightly here — fewer shoppers, more people with business that does not involve browsing. A narrow lane branches off between two welded containers.

On the left, a stall is hung with objects that do not belong on this station. Carved stones. A ring of dark metal. Something that looks like a compass but is not. A hand-lettered sign reads: PRECURSOR ARTEFACTS — AUTHENTICATED. The dealer is a thin human woman with prosthetic eyes that track you with unnerving precision.

Across the lane, a canteen has been set up in what was once a storage alcove — a counter, six stools, a pot of something that smells almost like coffee. Three scavengers sit at the counter, talking in low voices.

Farther down the lane, you can see a viewport set into the station's hull — one of the few on the middle ring that has not been plated over.

choices.text: Examine the artefact dealer's wares
Sit at the canteen and listen
Look out the viewport
Head back to the main market

s_market_artefact.text:
You stop at the stall and pick up the compass-like object. It is warm to the touch, which it should not be.

The dealer watches you with those prosthetic eyes. 'Precursor navigation aid,' she says. 'Third dynasty. Non-functional — the crystal matrix is cracked. Decorative only.' Her voice is flat and rehearsed.

You set it down and let your gaze drift across the other items. 'I'm looking for something specific. A Precursor navigation device. Intact.'

The change in her is immediate. Her hands go still. The prosthetic eyes dilate — an involuntary reflex, or the closest thing to one.

'I don't deal in intact Precursor nav systems,' she says. Too quickly. 'Those are Bureau-controlled items. Class one restricted.'

'I didn't say I was buying. I said I was looking.'

She is quiet for a long moment. When she speaks again, her voice is lower. 'If something like that existed on this station, and if someone were looking for it, I would tell that person two things. First: the Bureau already knows. Second: whatever is in the lower decks has been there a long time, and the things that guard it have been there longer.'

She picks up a cloth and begins polishing a stone. The conversation is over.

choices.text: Try the canteen
Check the viewport
Return to the main market

s_market_canteen.text:
You take a stool at the canteen counter and order whatever passes for coffee. It arrives in a dented tin cup — dark, bitter, and scalding. Good enough.

The three scavengers beside you are mid-conversation. You keep your eyes on your cup and your ears open.

'...went down to sub-four last week,' the one nearest to you is saying. A wiry woman with burn scars on her forearms. 'Made it as far as the atrium before the crawlers came out of the vents. Three of them. Lost my pack and barely made it back to the shaft.'

'Sub-four is gone,' says the man beside her. Big, bearded, missing two fingers on his left hand. 'Anything below three is crawler territory now. They nest in the ductwork — you can hear them clicking if you put your ear to the walls.'

The third scavenger, a young Qeth with a bandaged arm, shakes his head. 'The crawlers are not the problem. There is something else down there. Below sub-five. I heard it through the deck plates. Something large. Moving slowly.' He pauses. 'It was not mechanical.'

The other two go quiet. The woman finishes her coffee and does not order another.

At the far end of the counter, a man in clean clothes nurses a drink that is not coffee. He has been listening too.

You file the information away. Crawlers in the vents, something worse below. Useful to know.

choices.text: Talk to the man at the end of the counter
Ask the scarred woman for advice
Check the artefact stall
Look out the viewport
Return to the main market

s_market_fixer.text:
At the far end of the counter, the man sits alone. He is not a scavenger — his clothes are too clean, his hands too soft. When you sit down next to him, he does not look surprised.

'You have the look,' he says. 'Someone heading down, not up.' He takes a slow sip. 'I sell information. Specifics. Not the kind of thing Zara deals in — she trades in people and connections. I trade in movements. Patrol schedules. Imperial troop positions.'

He sets down his glass. 'Right now I can tell you this for free, because it is good for business when people survive: Malachar's advance team landed twenty minutes ago. Four soldiers, Imperial Enforcers, not regular Army. They are setting up a command post on the docking ring and they will be sweeping downward within the hour.'

He meets your eyes. 'If you are going to the lower decks, go now. Not in ten minutes. Now. Every minute you spend in this market is a minute they spend getting organized.'

The weight of the data chip in your pocket suddenly feels heavier.

choices.text: Ask about patrol schedules in the lower decks
Thank him and head back to the main market
Head straight for the north maintenance shafts
Head for the south cargo elevator

s_market_intel.text:
You lean closer. 'The patrol schedules,' you say. 'The automated ones in the lower decks. What do you know?'

He names a price. It is not cheap, but it is not as bad as Gruub's markup on the plasma pistol.

You pay. He speaks quickly and quietly.

'Station Authority used to run patrols on sub-levels three through six. Rotating shifts, four-man teams, standard six-hour cycles. When the garrison pulled out, the automated systems kept running for about a year. Security drones on fixed routes — predictable, avoidable if you know the timing.'

He draws a rough pattern on the counter with a wet finger. 'The drones cycle through each corridor every nine minutes. Wait at a junction, count to nine, and move when the drone passes. Simple.'

He wipes the pattern away. 'Below sub-six, there are no patrols. The drones do not go down there. Nothing goes down there. Take from that what you will.'

choices.text: Head back to the main market
Head for the north maintenance shafts
Head for the south cargo elevator

s_market_viewport.text:
The viewport is a metre-wide circle of reinforced glass, scratched and yellowed with age. You press your face close to it and look out.

New Babylon Station curves away from you in both directions — a vast torus of grey metal, pocked with running lights and the blue flicker of welding torches where repair crews work on the hull. Beyond the station, the stars are hard and white against absolute black.

And there, closing from the station's port quarter, you see it. A ship. Angular, dark, running without navigation lights. Imperial design — the kind of silhouette that makes civilian pilots change course. It is still distant, but it is not slowing down.

Malachar's corvette. The main force.

You watch it for ten seconds, maybe fifteen. It grows visibly larger in that time. Fast approach. No attempt at subtlety.

Your stomach tightens. The advance team is already here. The corvette is perhaps three hours out, maybe less. When it docks, the station will be locked down. No ships in or out. No exceptions.

You pull back from the viewport. Three hours to get down, find the Compass, and get out. The math is not encouraging.

choices.text: Browse the artefact stall
Try the canteen
Move toward the docking ring entrance
Return to the main market — no more time to waste

s_market_rumour.text:
The wiry woman with the burn scars catches your eye as you stand to leave the canteen. She glances at the other scavengers, then lowers her voice.

'You are going down. I can tell.' She turns her cup in her hands. 'Listen. Sub-level four, the main corridor splits at a junction marked with yellow paint. Left fork goes to the water processing plant — that is where the crawlers nest. You will hear them before you see them. Clicking, like someone tapping metal on metal.'

She rolls up her sleeve. The burn scars extend past her elbow. 'Right fork leads to the freight lifts. Most of them are dead, but one still has power — intermittent, unreliable. If you can get it working, it drops you straight to sub-six without passing through five.'

She pulls her sleeve back down. 'Sub-five is where I lost my crew. I do not recommend the scenic route.'

She picks up her cup and turns back to the counter. You have been given all the charity she has to offer.

choices.text: Check the artefact stall
Look out the viewport
Return to the main market

s_market_docking.text:
You follow the narrow lane past the viewport and find yourself at the edge of the market, where the corridor widens into a transit hub. From here you can see the entrance to docking ring three — a set of heavy blast doors flanked by faded Station Authority markings.

The blast doors are open. Through them, you catch a glimpse of activity — figures in dark uniforms moving with military precision, setting up portable barricades. Imperial Enforcers. They are not looking in your direction yet, but they are thorough, and they will be soon.

A station worker in a stained jumpsuit leans against the wall nearby, watching the Imperials with the flat expression of someone who has seen occupations before.

'They started twenty minutes ago,' she says, not looking at you. 'Checking IDs on the docking ring. They will be in the market within the hour.' She spits on the deck. 'Every time. Same routine. Lock the exits, sweep inward, take what they want.'

She pushes off the wall and walks away. You have seen enough. The noose is tightening, and you are still on the wrong side of it.

choices.text: Head back through the market
Head straight for the north maintenance shafts
Head for the south cargo elevator

s_harko_tour_1.text:
Instead of heading straight for the shafts, you take a longer route through Harko's territory. His heavy does not stop you — the deal has bought you a degree of freedom here.

The corridor opens into a wide maintenance bay that has been converted into a working operation. Cargo containers line the walls, some sealed, some open. Two of Harko's people — a man and a woman in matching grey jumpsuits — sit at a folding table checking manifests against a datapad. They glance up as you pass, assess you, and go back to work.

Beyond them, a container has been converted into a small market — no signs, no advertising, just a curtain across the opening and a steady trickle of people going in and out. Black market. The kind of place where you can buy things that do not officially exist on this station.

A third crew member leans against the wall near a heavy blast door, eating from a ration pack. He has a shotgun slung across his back and the relaxed posture of someone who knows exactly how much violence is appropriate in any given situation.

choices.text: Check out the black market container
Talk to the guard by the blast door
Check the map on the wall further down
Follow Harko's heavy through the corridors
Keep moving to the maintenance shafts

s_harko_tour_2.text:
You push through the curtain. Inside, the container is dim and cramped, lined with shelves of goods that fell off the back of a cargo shuttle. Stim-packs, ration packs, tools, ammunition, a rack of second-hand clothing. A Vellian woman sits on a crate behind a counter, arms folded.

Nothing you need that you do not already have. But the operation itself tells you something about Harko. This is not a smash-and-grab outfit. This is infrastructure. Supply chains. Organisation. The man is building something that will outlast any single deal.

You browse for a minute, letting your eyes adjust. On a back shelf, half-hidden behind a stack of water purification tablets, you notice a small shrine — a candle, a Qeth prayer stone, a faded photograph of someone's family. Even black marketeers need something to believe in.

You step back out into the corridor.

choices.text: Talk to the guard by the blast door
Head for the tunnel entrance
Head for the maintenance shafts

s_harko_tour_3.text:
You approach the guard by the blast door. He watches you come without straightening up — not hostile, just attentive.

'You're the one Harko just talked to,' he says. Not a question. 'Word travels fast down here.'

He jerks his chin toward the blast door behind him. 'That leads to the deep maintenance corridors. Sub-level three and below. We don't go past this point — Harko's orders. Too many things down there that eat shotgun pellets and keep coming.'

He takes another bite of his ration. 'Last crew that went deep was three months ago. Six of them. Two came back. One of those two stopped talking afterwards.' He chews. 'The other one works for Harko now. Night shift. Doesn't sleep much.'

He looks at you with something that might be pity. 'You're going down there alone?'

You do not answer. He nods as if you did.

choices.text: Check out the black market
Find the tunnel entrance
Head for the maintenance shafts

s_harko_tour_4.text:
Harko's heavy leads you down a side corridor that narrows as it goes. The lighting changes from the jury-rigged LED strips of Harko's territory to the original station fluorescents — most of them dead, a few flickering in irregular rhythms that make the shadows jump.

The corridor ends in a maintenance bay. Tools hang on wall racks, most of them coated in dust. A workbench holds the disassembled guts of an atmospheric processor. In the centre of the bay's floor, a hatch is set into the deck plates — a square metre of reinforced steel marked with strips of orange tape.

'That's your way down,' the heavy says. 'Maintenance shaft. Runs behind the outer hull for about fifty metres, then connects to the sub-level three service corridors.' He taps the orange tape with his boot. 'The tape is Harko's. Means the route has been checked in the last month. When you stop seeing orange tape, you're in unmapped territory.'

He pulls the hatch open. Below, a ladder descends into darkness. The air that rises from it is cold and smells of old metal and something faintly organic.

'Good luck,' he says. He does not sound like he means it.

choices.text: Descend into the shaft

s_harko_tour_5.text:
Further along the corridor, the wall panels have been removed, exposing the station's guts — pipes, cables, structural ribs. Someone has written on the bare metal in white paint: HARKO'S LAW — TAKE NOTHING YOU CAN'T CARRY, KILL NOTHING YOU CAN'T EAT.

Beneath the graffiti, a laminated sheet is bolted to the wall. A map of the sub-levels, hand-drawn and annotated. Most of it is labelled CLEARED or AVOID. Sub-level five is marked with a single word in red: NO.

You study the map for a minute. It confirms what you already suspected — the Void Compass coordinates point to sub-level six, in a section marked on no official chart. The map's annotations stop at sub-level four. Below that, Harko's people have not gone.

A crew member passes behind you, glances at the map, and keeps walking. This is old news down here. Everyone knows what is below. Nobody goes.

choices.text: Continue to the tunnel entrance
Head for the maintenance shafts

s_harko_escort.text:
Harko's heavy walks you back through the corridors toward the main thoroughfare. He is not talkative, but he is observant — he checks each junction before waving you through, and his hand stays near the shotgun on his hip.

As you pass through a narrow section between two cargo containers, he stops.

'Listen,' he says. Not to you — to the station. You stop and listen. Through the deck plates, through the walls, you can hear it: a low vibration, almost below the threshold of hearing. Not mechanical. Not the hum of power conduits or the rhythm of atmosphere recyclers. Something else.

It lasts five seconds, then stops.

The heavy looks at you. His expression has not changed, but his hand is now on the shotgun grip. 'That started about a month ago,' he says. 'Comes and goes. Nobody knows what it is. Nobody wants to find out.'

He starts walking again. You follow, and you do not ask questions.

choices.text: Check out the black market on the way past
Head straight for the maintenance shafts

s_sec_terminal.text:
Behind the main office, a narrow door leads to a back room — barely more than a closet. A terminal sits on a shelf bolted to the wall, its screen cracked but still displaying a cursor. Power is coming from somewhere — probably the same tapped line that keeps the lock panel running.

You tap a key. The screen flickers, then stabilises. The system is running on emergency backup — most functions are locked out, but the duty log directory is still accessible.

You scroll through the entries. Most are routine — patrol reports, maintenance tickets, noise complaints from the market. But the last few entries, dated three weeks before the garrison pulled out, are different.

'Anomalous readings from Research Lab 7, sub-level 6. Seismic activity inconsistent with station operations. Maintenance team dispatched — no report filed.'

'Second team dispatched to Lab 7. Team lead reports unusual electromagnetic interference. Equipment failures. Request for additional personnel denied by Station Command.'

The final entry is short: 'Lab 7 sealed by order of Station Commander. All personnel withdrawn from sub-levels 5 and 6. No further investigation authorised.'

Research Lab 7. Sub-level 6. That is where the data chip is pointing you.

choices.text: Check the evidence locker in the corner
Try the comms unit on the shelf
Leave the security office

s_sec_locker.text:
In the corner of the back room, a small evidence locker is bolted to the wall. Standard Station Authority model — reinforced steel, combination lock. The combination died with whoever set it.

The lock is good, but the hinges are not. You draw your vibro-knife and work the blade into the gap between the door and the frame. The molecular-edge oscillator whines as it bites into old metal. After thirty seconds of careful cutting, the hinge pins give way and the door swings open.

Inside: a dead officer's personal effects, sealed in a clear bag. A wedding ring. A data slate with a cracked screen. A photograph of two children standing in front of a viewport, stars behind them. A half-empty pack of cigarettes — real tobacco, worth a fortune on a station like this.

No weapons. No useful intelligence. Just the remains of someone's life, sealed away by procedure and forgotten by everyone.

You close the locker door and leave the contents where they are. Some salvage is not worth taking.

choices.text: Try the comms unit
Leave the security office

s_sec_comms.text:
On a shelf beside the terminal, a portable comms unit sits in its charging cradle. The unit is cracked and the antenna is bent, but a faint power light pulses on the side panel. Someone left it here in a hurry.

You pick it up and examine it. The transmitter is dead — no way to send. But the receiver might still work if you can patch the antenna connection.

choices.text: Attempt a repair with your repair kit
Leave the security office

s_sec_comms_fix.text:
You open your repair kit and get to work. The antenna connection is corroded but not broken — sealant paste and a new contact strip get it functional. You splice in a replacement capacitor from your spare boards and close the casing.

The comms unit hisses to life. Static, mostly. You cycle through frequencies — dead air, dead air, more dead air. Then, on a military band, you catch something. Fragments of a transmission, garbled by the station's hull.

'...Inquisitor confirms objective is sub-level six... research lab designation... full squad deploying to maintenance access points... authorization to use lethal force on any...'

The signal breaks up. You try to get it back, but it is gone — scattered by the station's metal bones.

Sub-level six. They know exactly where they are going. And lethal force authorisation means Malachar is not interested in witnesses.

You set the comms unit down. The information is not surprising, but hearing it in that clipped Imperial diction makes it real in a way that rumour does not.

choices.text: Check the evidence locker
Leave the security office

s_sec_exit.text:
You step out of the security office and pull the door shut behind you. The corridor is empty. From somewhere deeper in Harko's territory, you can hear voices and the clang of metal on metal — crew at work, maintaining the infrastructure that keeps this section livable.

The keycard sits in your pocket, a rectangle of hard plastic that represents access to places most people on this station have forgotten exist. Time to use it.

choices.text: Ask to speak with Harko
Head for the maintenance shafts

b_new_pa.text:
You stop. You stand in the dark between two freight containers and you listen.

The PA is still cycling. Malachar's voice plays on a loop — the same words, the same cadence, every ninety seconds. But it is the station's response that tells you what you need to know.

The hum of the cargo bay has changed. The maintenance drones that normally shuttle between container stacks have stopped moving — parked in their charging bays, obedient to some override signal you cannot see. The ventilation fans have shifted pitch, cycling faster. Someone has accessed the environmental controls. Someone with authority codes.

From somewhere in the upper decks, you hear the deep clang of a pressure bulkhead cycling shut. Then another. Then a third, further away. The station is being sealed, section by section, like a ship preparing for decompression.

This is not a raid. This is an occupation.

You have been on New Babylon long enough to know what happens to stations that attract Imperial attention. They do not recover. The people who live here — the maintenance crews, the cargo sorters, the families in the lower habitation blocks — they will be processed, relocated, or simply forgotten. The station will be stripped for intelligence value and then left to rot, this time without even the pretence of civilian governance.

The Void Compass pulses against your back. The Empire wants it. The Empire wants everything.

Time to move.

choices.text: You've heard enough

b_new_malachar_prep.text:
You move toward the cargo bay exit, but something is wrong. The space ahead is too quiet, too clean. The usual detritus of a working cargo bay — loose cable, discarded packing material, the accumulated grime of a station that no one maintains — has been cleared from the corridor. Sight lines opened up. Fields of fire established.

He knew you were coming.

The alarm you triggered in the lower decks told him everything he needed to know. Not just that someone was down there — that someone had found the artefact and was heading back up. His soldiers have been in position for minutes. The checkpoint ahead is not improvised. It is prepared.

You can see the marks on the deck plating where the portable barricade was dragged into place. The scorch marks on the wall where they tested their weapons' charge levels. Even the lighting has been adjusted — the overhead panels dimmed to put the glare in your eyes, not theirs.

Malachar has done this before. Many times. You are not the first scavenger to come up from the dark with something the Empire wanted. You will need to be smarter than the others were.

choices.text: Press forward

b_new_aftermath.text:
Malachar is on the deck. His rapier has powered down — the plasma edge guttering, fading, dying with its owner. The silver insignia at his throat catches the light.

You could run. You should run. Every second you stay here is a second his reinforcements get closer.

But something makes you pause. His coat has fallen open, and you can see the lining — not the standard-issue grey of Bureau field wear, but something older. Dark leather, hand-stitched, with a pattern you recognise. Precursor geometric work. The same angular lines you saw in the chamber below.

He was wearing it against his skin. A man who collected Precursor artefacts was literally wrapped in them.

A data tablet has slipped from his inside pocket, its screen cracked but still lit. You can see fragments of text — coordinates, star charts, a list of sites. The Void Compass was not his primary objective. It was a key to something larger. A network of Precursor installations, spread across the frontier, and Malachar had been mapping them for years. The compass would have completed his chart.

The tablet flickers and dies. Whatever was on it is gone — the cracked screen fading to black. But you saw enough. The compass is not just valuable. It is the centre of a web that someone in the Empire has been building quietly for a very long time.

The corridor ahead is still clear. For now.

choices.text: Take his insignia as proof and move
Leave him — run for the ship

b_new_station_free.text:
You are halfway to the docking ring when the station PA crackles again. But this time it is not Malachar's voice. It is the automated system — flat, synthetic, indifferent.

'Attention all personnel. Imperial corvette Relentless reporting drive malfunction. Docking bay seven lockdown protocols engaged. All Imperial personnel report to assigned stations.'

The PA cuts out. In the corridor ahead, two soldiers who were walking toward you stop, exchange a glance, and turn back the way they came. Recalled. Whatever happened to the Relentless — damage from station systems, sabotage, mechanical failure — it has pulled the Imperial presence inward, toward their own ship.

The station is not free. It will never be free again, not really. But for this moment, the corridors are empty and the way to the docking ring is clear. The bill for Malachar's arrogance has come due, and the Empire is paying it in confusion.

You move.

choices.text: Head for the docking ring

b_new_docking_ring.text:
The docking ring is chaos restrained. Imperial soldiers move in pairs through the main corridor, checking ident papers, scanning cargo manifests, establishing the bureaucratic architecture of occupation. But their attention is focused inward — toward the station, not toward the docks. They are securing what they have, not watching what leaves.

You keep to the service corridors. The maintenance passages that run behind the docking collar walls are narrow, unlit, and smell of recycled coolant. Nobody patrols them because nobody important uses them. You are not important. That is, for once, an advantage.

choices.text: Harko's people are still here — use their route
Navigate alone through the service corridors

b_new_harko_evac.text:
Harko's man is at the tunnel junction — the same orange jumpsuit, the same economy of movement. But this time he is not waiting for you. He is rigging the tunnel with demo charges, small shaped packages fixed to the structural supports at precise intervals.

'Harko's pulling out,' he says, without looking up from his work. 'Whole operation. The old man's been running this station for nine years and he knows when the house wins. Imperial corvette at the dock means it's over.'

He finishes the last charge and straightens up. 'He said to tell you — the deal stands. You got clear, his tunnels stay open until the charges blow. After that, this passage doesn't exist.' He checks a wrist-mounted timer. 'You've got four minutes.'

He moves past you, heading deeper into the tunnel network, toward whatever extraction route Harko has prepared for his own people. He does not look back.

Four minutes. The tunnel ahead leads to the docking ring.

choices.text: Move — fast

b_new_pistol_check.text:
The service corridor narrows where it passes behind docking collar seven — Malachar's own berth. Through a ventilation grille, you can see the boarding tunnel of the Relentless. Two guards in heavy armour, standing at parade rest. Professional. Alert.

The corridor beyond them leads to your docking collar. Twenty metres of open deck between the end of the service passage and the Sable Moth's boarding tunnel. Twenty metres with no cover, directly in the guards' sight line.

choices.text: Use the plasma pistol — drop them at range before they react
Wait for a shift change and slip past

b_new_pistol_shot.text:
The plasma pistol's advantage has always been range. A vibro-knife needs you close. A pistol needs you accurate.

You brace against the corridor wall, sight through the grille, and fire twice. The first shot catches the near guard in the chest plate — he staggers back, armour smoking. The second takes the far guard in the shoulder as he turns. Neither goes down, but they are hurt, disoriented, and looking the wrong way.

You are through the service hatch and across the open deck before they recover. A plasma bolt scorches the wall behind you as you dive into the Sable Moth's boarding tunnel. Close. But close is good enough.

choices.text: Board the Sable Moth

b_new_dock_luck.text:
You wait. The guards hold position, disciplined and still. Minutes pass. Then one of them touches his earpiece — new orders, or a shift rotation. Both turn toward the Relentless's airlock.

This is your window. You move fast, staying low, feet quiet on the deck plating. The twenty metres feel like twenty miles.

test_luck.lucky_text: You make it. The gap closes behind you — the guards returning to their post — but you are already in the shadow of the Sable Moth's boarding tunnel. They never saw you. Your heart is hammering, but your hands are steady.
test_luck.unlucky_text: You are halfway across when one of the guards turns back. He sees you. A shout, a bolt of plasma searing the deck plating at your feet. You sprint the last ten metres, taking a glancing hit across your ribs that drops you to one knee. You drag yourself into the boarding tunnel before the second shot comes.

b_new_sable_moth.text:
The Sable Moth sits in docking collar nine, exactly where you left it. For a moment you just stand there, looking at it through the reinforced viewport of the boarding tunnel.

She is not a beautiful ship. A battered Kestrel-class light freighter, twenty years past her service date, held together by aftermarket parts and a maintenance schedule that is more prayer than engineering. The hull plating is mismatched — three different shades of grey where panels have been replaced over the years. One of the manoeuvring thrusters has a visible weld scar from a docking accident you prefer not to discuss.

But the engines work. The FTL drive is sound. And right now, she is the most beautiful thing you have ever seen.

You cycle the airlock and step aboard. The cockpit air smells like home — recycled atmosphere, old coffee, the faint ozone tang of electronics that run too hot. You run the pre-flight sequence from memory. Fuel cells: sixty-two per cent. Drive coils: charged. Navigation: online.

Through the cockpit viewport, you can see New Babylon Station spread out below you. Imperial shuttles are moving between the docking bays. Running lights on the Relentless blink in the middle distance. The station that was your home for two years is becoming something else, and you are watching it happen in real time.

choices.text: Wait one minute — Voss might still make it to the airlock
Use the stim-pack before launch
Engage the FTL drive

b_new_wait_voss.text:
You sit in the pilot's seat with your hand on the drive engagement lever and you wait.

Sixty seconds. That is what you give him. One minute, measured by the cockpit chronometer, while Imperial patrols sweep the docking ring and the window for escape narrows with every heartbeat.

Forty seconds. Nothing. The airlock sensors show no movement in the boarding tunnel.

Thirty seconds. A squad of soldiers passes the viewport, heading toward the adjacent docking collar. They do not look your way. Yet.

Fifteen seconds. The comm unit crackles — static, or maybe a carrier signal too weak to resolve. You cannot tell.

Five seconds. The airlock proximity sensor chirps. A single contact, moving fast, in the boarding tunnel. The airlock cycles.

Voss comes through the hatch like a man who has been running for his life, because he has. One arm is bound with a torn sleeve. His jacket is gone. He has a plasma burn across his left shoulder that he is pretending does not exist.

He drops into the co-pilot's seat, breathing hard, and looks at you.

'You waited.'

'Don't make it a habit.'

choices.text: Use the stim-pack before launch
Engage the FTL drive

b_new_bay_approach.text:
The far side of the cargo bay is different from the side you entered. Here, the containers are stacked higher and closer together, forming narrow canyons of corrugated steel. The air is warmer — you are closer to the inhabited decks, closer to the life support cores that still function.

You can hear the station around you now. Not the dead silence of the lower decks, but the living noise of a place full of people who have just been told their world has changed. Muffled voices behind bulkhead doors. The distant whine of a lift car moving too fast. Someone is running, somewhere above you — boots on metal, urgent but not panicked. Not yet.

Through a gap in the container wall you catch a glimpse of the main concourse. Imperial soldiers are visible at the far end — grey uniforms, sidearms, the practised stillness of professionals establishing a perimeter. They have not reached this section yet. But they will.

The exit is ahead. Beyond it, the corridor that leads to the upper decks and the docking ring. Between you and it stands one man who has crossed Imperial space to take what you are carrying.

You check your gear. You steady your breathing. You move.

choices.text: Step out to face what's waiting

e_eng_1.text:
The corridor widens into a vaulted space that must have been impressive once. The sign above the entrance reads ENGINEERING SECTION 4 — PROPULSION RESEARCH in faded stencil. Below it, someone scratched a later addition: DECOMMISSIONED — NO ENTRY BY ORDER OF STATION AUTHORITY.

Station Authority hasn't existed for three years. You duck under the sign.

The section opens into a long gallery with workbenches bolted to both walls. Most of the equipment is gone — stripped for parts long ago — but the benches themselves remain, scarred and stained by decades of use. Above them, cable runs sag from the ceiling like dead vines. The air is noticeably colder here, and each breath comes out in a faint cloud.

Ahead, the gallery continues into darkness. To your right, a heavy door stands ajar, marked DRIVE CORE TEST BAY. To your left, a narrow passage runs between conduit banks toward what looks like a maintenance junction.

choices.text: Enter the Drive Core Test Bay
Follow the passage toward the maintenance junction
Continue through the gallery toward the far end

e_eng_2.text:
The Drive Core Test Bay is a circular room dominated by a massive cradle in its centre — the mounting point for experimental drive cores during testing. The cradle is empty now, its clamps open like the fingers of a dead hand. Scorch marks radiate outward from it across the floor and up the walls. Whatever they tested last, it did not go well.

The walls are covered in instrumentation panels, all dead. But on a workbench near the door, you find something intact: a set of handwritten research notes, pinned under a broken calibration tool. The handwriting is precise, small, the kind that belongs to someone who writes reports for a living.

The notes describe test protocols for 'artefact-integrated propulsion' — attempts to use Precursor technology as a component in human drive systems. The results are clinical: 'Artefact refuses integration. Rejects all bonding agents. Surface temperature increases during contact with human-manufactured components. Recommend discontinuation of programme.'

The last entry is dated eleven years ago. Below it, in different handwriting, a single line: 'Bureau assumes control of all Precursor materials. Effective immediately.'

The Bureau. Malachar's people. They were here a decade ago, stripping the station of anything Precursor-related. The Void Compass was the one they missed.

choices.text: Search the bay more thoroughly
Return to the gallery

e_eng_notes.text:
You work your way around the test bay, opening panels and checking storage compartments. Most are empty — the Bureau was thorough. But in a sealed locker beneath the main console, you find a binder of handling protocols for Precursor materials.

The protocols are detailed and deeply cautious. They describe Precursor artefacts as 'non-responsive to electromagnetic analysis,' 'thermally anomalous,' and 'resistant to all forms of mechanical stress testing.' One section catches your eye: a procedure for Precursor chambers. Pressure plates, light-carrying channels, geometric triggers. The researchers had mapped the mechanisms. They could not understand them, but they could describe them.

You tear out the relevant pages and fold them into your jacket. The knowledge won't change what you find, but it might change how fast you recognise it.

choices.text: Return to the gallery

e_eng_3.text:
The gallery narrows as you move deeper into Engineering Section 4. The workbenches give way to heavy machinery — reactor test rigs, shielding assemblies, things bolted to the floor with industrial fasteners thicker than your arm. Everything is cold and dark and covered in a fine grey dust that no one has disturbed in years.

Then you hear it. A low mechanical whine, cycling up and down. Not station systems — too regular, too deliberate. Something is active ahead.

You round a corner and see it: a security drone, wedged in the junction where Engineering Section 4 meets the main lower deck corridor. It is an old model — Station Authority hardware, not Imperial — hovering half a metre off the ground, its sensor array sweeping back and forth. Its chassis is dented and one of its stabiliser fins is bent, but its weapons port is intact and tracking.

It has been here since before the station fell. Still running its patrol route. Still following orders from an authority that no longer exists.

choices.text: Try to slip past while its sensor sweeps away from you
Destroy it
Use the EMP grenade to disable it
Go back — find another route

e_eng_drone_luck.text: You time the sweep. The drone's sensor array rotates on a five-second cycle — three seconds scanning forward, two seconds returning. You wait for the return sweep and move.

test_luck.lucky_text: You cross the junction in the blind spot between sweeps, pressing yourself flat against the far wall. The drone's sensor passes over the space you occupied a second ago and finds nothing. You exhale and keep moving.
test_luck.unlucky_text: Your boot catches a cable on the floor. The drone's sensor snaps toward the vibration and locks on. A targeting laser paints your chest red. You throw yourself sideways as the first shot scorches the wall behind you. No choice now.

e_eng_drone_fight.text: The drone registers you as hostile and drops into combat mode. Its chassis tilts forward, weapons port deploying, and it fires. The shot goes wide — the bent stabiliser fin throws off its aim — but the next one won't miss.

e_eng_drone_emp.text:
You pull the pin on the EMP grenade and roll it across the floor toward the drone. The detonation is a sharp blue-white flash and a sound like a transformer exploding. The drone drops out of the air instantly, hitting the deck with a clang that echoes through the corridor. Its sensor array goes dark. Its weapons port locks mid-rotation.

The lights in this section die too — everything electronic within range is dead. You navigate by touch for twenty metres until the station's systems pick up again.

choices.text: Continue through the junction

e_eng_beyond.text:
Beyond the drone junction, the corridor connects to the main lower deck route. You are past Engineering Section 4 and closer to Research Lab 7. The waypoint arrow on your data chip adjusts, pointing ahead and slightly down.

From here, you can see the approaches to the lab. The corridor is wider, the ceiling higher — pre-Imperial architecture showing through the station's later modifications. The walls are smoother, the angles cleaner. You are entering the oldest part of the station.

Ahead, you notice a side passage with faded writing on the walls. To your right, the corridor floor looks wrong — the deck plates are discoloured. And from further on, you can hear boots.

choices.text: Head for Research Lab 7
Check the side passage with the writing
The deck plates ahead look wrong — proceed carefully
Imperial patrol ahead — assess the situation

e_eng_junction.text:
The maintenance junction is a cramped node where six conduit runs converge. A terminal is mounted on the wall — old but powered, its screen casting a faint green glow across the pipes and cables. Station infrastructure monitoring. Still running on backup power after all these years.

You tap the screen. It takes a moment to respond, then displays a schematic of the lower decks — crude, outdated, but functional. You can see the corridors you've been walking through, and ahead of you, marked in red, Research Lab 7.

The terminal also shows active power signatures. Most of the lower deck is dead, but three clusters of activity are lit up: the lab itself, a node near where you heard the Imperial comms, and a third signature you don't recognise — deeper, below the main deck level, pulsing in a slow rhythm that doesn't match any station system you know.

You study the schematic. There's a service route from here that bypasses the main corridor entirely, running through old coolant infrastructure directly to the lab approach.

choices.text: Take the service route to Research Lab 7
Return to the gallery and continue exploring

e_flood_1.text:
The passage slopes downward. After twenty metres the reason becomes clear: coolant. A burst pipe somewhere above has been leaking for years, and the lowest section of this corridor is flooded. The liquid is ankle-deep, dark, and slightly viscous — reactor coolant, non-toxic but unpleasant. It is cold enough to feel through your boots.

Conduit banks run along both walls above the waterline, their cables sagging and corroded. The ceiling is low here, forcing you to duck. Your light reflects off the dark surface of the coolant in a way that makes the corridor look longer than it is.

The passage continues ahead, the water deepening slightly. You can see a junction thirty metres on where the corridor branches. From somewhere ahead — hard to tell exactly where, with the acoustics distorted by the water — you hear a rhythmic dripping that isn't quite random enough to be natural.

choices.text: Wade through toward the junction
Go back — this route is too exposed

e_flood_2.text:
The water reaches mid-calf at the deepest point. It is achingly cold, and each step sends ripples ahead of you that make the reflections on the ceiling shift and slide. The dripping sound resolves as you get closer — it is coming from a cracked pipe above the junction, drops falling into the flooded corridor with metronomic regularity.

At the junction, the water shallows. To your left, a passage slopes upward and dries out — you can see station lighting ahead, the first you've seen in this section. To your right, the corridor continues at the same level, flooded, and ends at a sealed bulkhead with a faded sign: LOWER RESEARCH WING — AUTHORISED PERSONNEL ONLY.

The bulkhead has a keycard reader. Its indicator light blinks amber — powered but locked.

choices.text: Take the left passage — upward toward the light
Use the security keycard on the bulkhead
Try to force the bulkhead open

e_flood_force.text:
You brace yourself against the corridor wall and pull at the bulkhead. The mechanism is old and the water has corroded the lower track. The door shifts — a centimetre, two — then jams. You pull harder. Something gives, and the door lurches open with a grinding shriek that echoes through the flooded corridor.

The noise carries. Sound travels strangely through water and metal, and a shriek like that could be heard two sections away. You freeze and listen. Nothing comes back. But that doesn't mean nothing heard it.

Beyond the bulkhead, a short corridor slopes upward out of the flood zone and opens into a dry antechamber. This is a back entrance to the research wing.

choices.text: Enter the research wing

e_flood_exit.text:
You climb the passage, grateful to be out of the water. The floor dries under your boots and the temperature rises marginally — still cold, but not the bone-deep chill of the flooded section.

The passage connects to the main lower deck corridor. You recognise the intersection from the station schematics. Research Lab 7 is ahead, through the wider corridor with the pre-Imperial architecture. A half-open door to your left shows a room with projected maps on the wall — someone important is inside.

choices.text: Head for Research Lab 7
You hear voices ahead — Imperial soldiers
Look through the half-open door — an officer is inside

e_flood_lab.text:
The keycard reader chirps and the amber light turns green. The bulkhead disengages with a heavy clunk and slides open, water spilling through the gap. Beyond it, a short corridor slopes upward out of the flood zone and opens into a dry antechamber.

This is a back entrance to the research wing. The walls here are cleaner, better maintained — someone kept this section in working order long after the rest of the lower decks were abandoned. Research equipment lines the walls: sealed storage cases, environmental monitoring stations, a decontamination arch that flickers weakly as you pass through it.

Ahead, through a reinforced glass partition, you can see the circular chamber of Research Lab 7. The Void Compass is visible on its pedestal, pulsing with that slow silver light.

choices.text: Enter Research Lab 7
Search the antechamber first

e_lab_ante.text:
The antechamber was a staging area for researchers entering Lab 7. Lockers line one wall, most hanging open and empty. Environmental suits on hooks, cracked and faded. A clipboard on the workbench holds a yellowed form: PRECURSOR MATERIALS HANDLING — DAILY LOG.

The log entries span three months. The handwriting changes every few days — different researchers on rotation. Most entries are brief and clinical. 'No change in artefact luminescence.' 'Temperature variance within expected parameters.' 'Artefact non-responsive to all stimuli.'

The last entry is longer. Different tone. 'The compass responded today. First confirmed response in 14 months of observation. Dr. Vasquez was running a standard proximity scan when the silver veins brightened. She reported a sensation of directional awareness — a pull, she called it. Toward galactic north-east. The sensation lasted approximately four seconds before the artefact returned to baseline. Dr. Vasquez has been removed from the project per Bureau protocol 7.'

Removed. You notice the word. Not reassigned. Removed.

choices.text: Enter Research Lab 7

e_lab_approach.text:
The service route is a crawl through old coolant infrastructure — narrow, lightless, and full of pipes that radiate residual heat from systems that shut down years ago. You move on hands and knees in places, navigating by the schematic you memorised at the terminal.

After ten minutes you emerge through an access panel into a corridor you recognise from the station map. Research Lab 7 is ahead. You approached from a direction the Imperial patrols aren't covering — they set up their cordon along the main routes, not the service tunnels.

You can hear them, faintly. Boots on metal, comms chatter. But between you and the lab, the corridor is empty.

choices.text: Enter Research Lab 7

e_imp_disarray.text:
The corridors beyond the sensor relay are different now. You can hear it — the Imperial comms net has fractured. Fragments of confused transmissions echo from side passages. Soldiers calling in positions that don't match the terrain. Orders countermanded, then countermanded again. The false sensor data is doing its work.

You pass a junction and see two soldiers arguing over a portable display. One is jabbing at the screen, insisting this corridor should read as cleared. The other is looking at the actual corridor — dark, unmapped, nothing like what his display shows. Neither of them sees you.
